CURRENT AFFAIRS,

There was a very interesting function at St. Kevin’s
Park, Kilmacud, on Wednesday the 19th inst. St.
Kevin’s Park is about two miles from Dundrum rail-
way station. The reposeful scenery of the place had a
welcome and soothing effect on our irritated nerves—
for there were three ladies of the vulgar “classy "’ type
in the compartment of the railway carriage in which
we travelled from Harcourt Street to Dundrum. Let
us hasten to add they were not going to St. Kevin's
Park. Oh,. those loud talking vulgar females who will
shout about their business to the annoyance of peaceful
‘and law-abiding fellow-passengers.  Some Percy or
Berty is always going to, in, or about to come ba_c}(
from Switzerland. Auntie is staying somewhere with
«mothaw ”’ for a month, and Gwendoline is looking
«so mice’’ after her trip to London; the Haw-Haws
have just gone to Greystones for a change, and uncle
is expected back from Torquay in a week. Vic-tor-ia

“is on a visit to the de la Flimsies—the de la Flimsies
are “awfully nice” people, and Vic-tor-ia is “simply
- chawming *’—thus do these inhuman documents bellow
forth in strident voices, making many a railway journay:

a nerve-racking experience for sensible, mannerly and
quiet people. It is in vain one puts his finger in his
car, the rasping swagger about uncle and auntie and
Bertie and about where they are “ stopping ’” and “ stay-
ing ”’ would penetrate a deal board. We sighed with
relief when we stepped on the platform at Dundrum,
and when the chroniclers of the “stayings” and “stop-
pings ”’ of Bertie and auntie steamed off to Greystones

Two special articles on St. Kevin’s, Kilmacud, have
already appeared in the Leaprr, so that our readers
are fairly familiar with the work and objects of this
excellent institution which owes its existence mainly t
the initiative and energy of Father Glendon, O.P. St
Kevin's House was started as a holiday house foi
Catholic ladies, more particularly for ladies engaged
all the year round in business. That is its primary
purpose still; but it is also now a technical school for
Catholic girls. In the May of last year the training
school of domestic economy for servants sent in by the
Dublin County Council was started; and in Septembes
last a training college for domestic economy for young
ladies was established. The Lord Mayor presented cer-
tificates to six of the County Council students who had
passed the examination. The terms for holiday resi-
dence are very moderate, and when the attractions of
St. Kevin’s House are more generally known, the capa-
city of this branch of this beneficent establishment
amongst the Dublin mountains will be taxed to its
utmost. Ladies can have board and residence in this
delightful holiday retreat for as small a weekly fee as
12s. 6d., and this includes use of sitting rooms, light,
bathrooms, library and games, and the board includes
four meals a day. If ladies requiring a holiday rest can
obtain it on more economical terms than that—well let
them take it and welcome. This establishment deserves
all the support that it can get, and it is important that
it should obtain a superabundance of support, for it
may be regarded as a pioneer enterprise in a department
of national activity that has been allowed to lie fallow
until the Dominicans put the first furrow in it. The
technical school in connection with the Dublin County
Council for the training of domestic servants, is not
only a long felt, but a crying, want, in this country. i
The school of domestic economy for ladies destined in
the ordinary course to preside over homes in the future,
need, to say the least, make no apology for its existeuce
in this country; for “accomplishments’’ on the piano
or guitar, standing alone, are poor assets to any young
Trish gentleman who has to support a wife and family
on thirty-five shillings a week. The marriage rate in
Ireland is exceptionally low; it may run counter to
the keen susceptibilities of the Irish national penny
novelette readers, but nevertheless we make bold to
suggest that the marriage rate would go up if TIrish
girls were half as well able to keep house as they are
to strum a piano.

The Castlebellingham Feis was a great success last
Sunday. Canon Ryan, P.P., of Tipperary, in the
course of his address, said:—*God bless the Gaelic
Movement whether their Feis was held on a Sunday or
Monday. God bless what made those days, days of in-
nocent recreation. He did not think that the Almighty
frowned on them that day because it happened to be
His day.”’

These remarks had a special significance in connection
with the Castlebellingham Feis; for in the hospitable
columns of the so-called “ Church of Ireland’’ Gazette
the Rev. James B. Leslie, Kilsaren Rectory, Castlebel-
lingham, denounced the holding of the Feis on a Sun-
day. The fact that it had been announced that Lord .

Ballinagoul, and in fact the whole P-arish of Ring 
and old Parish is the most Irish-speaking part of ·the 
Co. Waterford. It is within three miles of Dungarvan 
by boat, four by the Ounnigor, and eight by road; so 
it is convenient for visitors to Dungarvan who wish to 
·get a spoken knowledge of our once native language. 
A limited numbPr of visitors can find suitable lodgings 
in Ring itself, and at present on Irish class is carried on 
in Ballinagoul every evening under the charge of Mr. P. 
Kiely, G. L . Organiser. 
Anybody wishing to com-
bine business with pleasure in this respect should visit 
Dungarvan and Ballinagou_l during the summer holidays.

language sense, for as will be seen by their announce~

~on the Great 8. and W. Railway of depreciated profits.
The methods adopted by Brother Goulding’s railway
for attempting to recover the excess fare were under-
~ band and mean, and unworthy of even a trick-o’-the-
loop man.  But with all the meanness and underhand
business capacity displayed the company failed in their
object. They found out that a person of the same name
as the passenger who refused to pay what he considered
the unfair and inequitable excess was employed in a
certain firm in England. The mighty Great Southern
and Western Railway of Ireland wrote off to the head
of this firm to the effect that an employee of his had
travelled from Cork to Dublin with a ticket which did
not entitle him to do so, and that they would be glad
if he would see that the excess fare was remitted.
They failed. It is believed that this great railway also
wrote to another firm in England in which another per-
son of the same name is employed, but here also the
great railway failed. Now Brother Goulding’s railway
company have the Irish address of the passenger, and if
they only have the straightforwardness and pluck to
summon him he, we hear, will be only too pleased to
meet them. But this great company evidently prefers
sneaking round writing on chance to English firms in
which some man or other of the same name is employed.

An Irish concert and entertainment in aid of the
widow and children of the late Mr. John M‘Carthy, a
pioneer worker of the Gaelic League in Dublin, will be
held on Saturday next in Banba Hall, Rutland Square.
‘We are assured that an excellent programme has been
arranged, and the commendable object for which the
entertainment is being organised will help to gather a
large audience for the occasion.

On more than one occasion it has given us pleasure
to refer to the support given to Irish manufacture by
firms located in England. Indeed, some firms catering
for the Irish resident across the water make a bid in
this matter to be more Irish than the Irish themselves.
Messrs. Corrigan and French of London, who write to

% us we notice on Irish notepaper, the printing on which
was also done in Ireland, have forwarded us a copy of a
Genealogical Map which they are presenting to every
purchaser of one of their suits of Irish material. This

. map, which is neatly got up, shows the place of origin
< - of the various family names of Ireland according to
country and district, and also contains an alphabetical

list designed for handy reference.

| Nearly forty students are now attending the Munster
1 Training College, seven having joined within the past
week. The work continues to progress in a manner
- highly satisfactory to all concerned. A few days ago
‘the institution was visited by Dr. Windle, President
of the Queen’s College, Cork, and himself a member of
~ Cotdirte na Muian. He was introduced by the
=5 Rev. Fater Hurley, P.P., President of the Com-
* mittee of the Munster Training College. Demonstra-
tion and criticism lessons on the method of using pic-
tures (of the seasons, city and rural occupations, etc.),
for teaching Irish conversation are now included in
the programme. The London Gaelic League and the
Keating Branch Gaelic League (Dublin) have lent the
_ College some excellent pictures for this purpose. The
“Banba ’’ charts for teaching the Irish verb presented
by the Keating Branch are found useful also. Pro-
bably one of the most welcome lessons in the day’s pro-
‘gramme is that given to Irish traditional singing, Miss
Annie O'Reilly of Macroom, being the teacher. The
Scopuiveacc which are held every night are de-
lightful, many old songs and stories not previously
“ printed being given by the students and also by people
rom the surrounding districts.

‘The Trish Post Office Clerks’ Union now has a mem-
ship of almost 1,100.  Within the last year the
er of branches increased from 15 to 64, and the
ership of the Central Branch more than doubled.
he annual report we read :—There
ble room for' improvement in the man-

ner in which postmasterships are filled in this country.
While some little improyement has taken place as a
consequence of the vigilance of your Association, there
still continues what appears to be a fixed determination
to give very few of these positions to the postal service
of Ireland.”” We have helped in the showing up of a
good many post office importations, and we would be
glad if our post office readers would let us know at
once of every case of importation that occurs.

Mr. Normile in proposing a resolution protesting
against undue importation of British postal officials,
said that:—“In 1900 there was appointed to the post-
mastership of Castlerea a clerk from South Shields, at
a salary of £180 a year, and in the same year the
Postmastership of Kilmallock was given to another
Englishman, at a salary of £140 a year. During that
year no Irish official got an appointment outside Ireland.
In 1901, Kilrush, Derry, Youghal, and Buttevant
offices, worth in all £790, were given to Englishmen,
while against that, one Irish official got an English
post office at a salary of £150. In 1902 several im-
portant Irish offices were given to Englishmen at
salaries amounting in all to £1,770. In that year mo
Irish official was given a post office outside Ireland.
During those three years Englishmen got post-offices to
the amount of £2,880, while only one Irishman, at a
salary of £150, got an office outside Ireland.’”

A week in Killarney with locomotion (including Rail-
way Fares, Coach Drives and Boating) and also acco-
modation for the inclusive cost of £2 19s. 3d., seems
particularly cheap. Anybody who would like to take
advantage of this offer should be interested in Messrs.
Thomas Cook and Son’s announcement, especially those
who intend to visit the Munster Feis, commencing on
the 6th of August.

Brother Goulding, bearer of the Inner Guard Sword
of the Free and Accepted Masons of Ireland, Frederick
C. Pilkington of the National Assurance Co., not be-
loved of many widows and orphans, and their colleagues
are tricky.  They apparently instructed Francis B.
Ormsby, of a Protestant Orphan Home, and like-
wise Secretary of the Great Sourface Railway, to rend
out circulars to the poor shareholders of this depreciated
company for proxies. The tricky directors of this com- -
pany sent for the proxies before they sent out the re-
port.  Brother Goulding, of the Sword, was :of
born yesterday. He would like to have his hands on
the proxies before the shareholders had their eyes on
the report of the depreciated company of which
Frederick C. Pilkington, of the National Assurance
Company is a director. Brother Goulding, bearer of
the Inner Guard Sword of the Free and Accepted Masons
of Ireland, Brother Goulding who apologised by letter
for not attending the recent flare up of the Orangemen
at the Rotunda, would like to have the votes before he
showed the accounts. Perhaps we are impertinent, if
not “obscurant,”’ in asking for the accounts before
Ormsby requested the proxies.

The order of events for the forthcoming Munster Feis
has been decided on, and will be found in our adver-
tising columns. The number of entries received is large,
and there has been a decided increase iry the entries for
the Language Competitions. The Cork Workingmen’s
Prize Band has been secured for the days of the Feis,
and evening promenade concerts and open-air Irish
plays have been arranged for. Arrangements for ban-
quet on the evening of Munster Conference, Monday,
August 6, have been completed, and tickets may now
be had by application to the secretaries. The Feis will
be opened by the Most Rey. Dr. Mangan, who has
always displayed a strong interest in the Language and
Industrial Movements. Addresses will be delivered by
prominent Gaelic Leaguers on the days and evenings
of Feis. : 3 )

National teachers will be particularly interested in
the division on the motion of the Most Rev. Dr. Foley—
““ That any school upon which the effect of Rule 127 (b)
would be to reduce the salary of an existing teacher shall
be exempt from the operation of that Rule during such
teacher’s tenure of office.”” Major Dease, Rev. Dr.
Evans, Mr. Molloy, and Mr. Morell supported the
Bishop’s motion, and the following—and the teachers
should note it well—voted against:—The Lord Chief
Baron, the notorious ‘‘ Trinity ** Catholic and President,

5 T again urge shareholders in public companies to com-
pel their directors to avail of the Forged Transfer Acts,
and thus give solidity to their securities, confidence to
their shareholders and protection from loss by designing
fraudulent men.

The frequent revelations of these frauds upon inno-
cent victims who have for years rested satisfied that
their incomes were beyond dispute, and by the death
of a co-trustee have been awakened to the reality that
their investment was worthless because the instrument
(the transfer) conveying it was a fraud, have deeply
startled investors generally.

There is now a shelter for all public companies in a
new form of insurance, by which for a sum varying
from 6d. to 2d. per £100, the company guarantees any
loss by an invalid or forged instrument.

The half-yearly meetings of several Corporations are
now about being held, and an opportune time is avail-
able for bringing this very serious matter before the
directors, and I cannot too importunately endeavour
to persuade all who desire peaceful minds upon the
validity of their securities to insist that a means so
simple and inexpensive shall be availed of, and the ad-
vantages offered by this insurance company at'such a
nominal expenditure shall* be, without further delay,
put in operation.

THE CLONGOWNIAN, THE CHEIEF BARON,
AND OTHER MATTERS.

HE Clongownian has been regularly reviewed in
these columns; yet we have to buy our copies,
for the able editor is so modest that, as far as we are
concerned, we have to pay tariff of one shilling before
we are in a position to thrust fame upon him. Last
year it will be remembered that one of the show contri-
butors flippantly bragged in an article that appeared
in the Clongownian that he ate roast turkey on one
Christmas Eve—a solemn fast day of the Church. We
feel sure that the able editor has been watchful that no
fast-breaking “ copy *’ slipped him this time.

On the face of it there is a palpable attempt this year
to put as much of an Irish countenance on the publica-
tion as pressing considerations of ‘respectability >’
would stand. Amongst the contents is an article in the
Irish language. But what value is a partly Irish face if
the heart is still West British? Fow about the teach-
ing of Irish in the College? Our information is, that
there is not an Intermediate class carried on in the Col-
lege that does not get, at least, six hours’ Latin ‘teach-
ing and seven and a half hours Latin study in the
week. Bach class gets about six hours for French.
There is only 14 hours given to Irish during the week ;
and there is no fixed study for Irish. Ouly 12 out of
the entire roll of students presented themselves for
examination in Irish if our information be accurate.
French songs are sung at the concerts, but the “ tone ”’
of Clongowes is never, we hear, shocked by an Irish
song.  However, despite the adverse environment of
the Lord Ckief Baron’s dlma Mater, an attempt was
made by some students to arrange an Irish-speaking
table in the Refectory during the year, but the project
was sat upon. Having lifted this little corner of the
curtain on Clongowes, we may return to the Clongow-
nan.

Let it be put down in all honesty that, as a publica-
tion, it is miles ahead of the insipid Castleknock
Chronicle.  We read that on March 17th last, that “a
dramatic and musical entertainment brought the day
to a close.”” The full particulars are conspicuous by
their absence. The first on the list of “ The Past’’ is
a Mr. W. P. Kelly. We read:—“On leaving Clon-
gowes he ‘crammed’ for the army in Dublin, and in
1867 passed into the Royal Military Academy, Wool-
wich, obtaining seventeenth place in the Competitive
Examination.”” In 1867, when this man was passing
into the Royal Military Academy, a good many other
men, not educated in Clongowes, were passing into her
Majesty’s jails. Though a one-time lieutenant of the
British “ millingtery ’ is given first place in “The
Past,”’ men of a totally different calibre are not boy-
cotted. Away back we find paragraphs headed
“Thomas Francis Meagher’’ and “Richard D’Alton Wil-
liams.”” One gratifying thing is that we looked in
vain through “The Past’ for any reference to any
student having gone to the bogus kind of a University.
where Herrings 156 and 16 were branded under interest-
ing as well as suspicious circumstances. It is a sign of
progress that even the Clongownian has evidently come
to the conclusion that the time has come to turn its
back on the burst bubble of College Green..

In the report of the College Union the now notorious
remarks of the President, the Lord Chief Baron, a
“Tyinity ”” Catholic, are set out but not as they were
reported in the Freeman. We had written the fore-

going sentence after glancing through the speech as re-

ported in the Clongownian when our eye caught the

following at the end of the report:— Freeman, June

Kelleher had a great success, and obtained higher marks

tained before.

As in every Fellowship exam-
ination they probably would have four or five unsuc-
cessful candidates for the one successful candidate, and 
as mathematics was a subject which in Trinity College 
counted higher than any other subject in the competi-
tion for Fellowships, necessarily every competitor for a

Now why did the 
able editor who nimbly dropped out "A British m:li-

exercise their minds over this interesting puzzle. 
Are 
we coming within measurable distance of the time when

not to say President, of even such a " tame " ~orpor:1,-
tion as the Olongowes College Union? 
Let us say, though we have only .read a small portion 
of it, and do not intend to read any more, that the 
magazine appears to be a decided improvement on last 
year-that of course is not saying much. 
The editorial 
introduction is decidedly a poor thing; whoever the 
editor may be he is certainly not a born humourist, aud 
he has his work cut out for him if he would attain ex-
cellence in the writing trade. It is a· good job for him 
that his publication only comes out· once a year.

“ An estate which at present yields £6,700 a year ’’—

terests, given away to one institution or another with-

called English Schools with the National System, which

Erasmus  Smith, Esq., of Tondon, was one
of the adventurers. In  the year 1642,
an  Act was passed in the English  Parlia-
ment confiscating 2,500,000 acres of land in Ireland on
account of the Rebellion of 1641. These lands were
offered to Englishmen who would contribute to raise and
pay a private army for the subduing of the rebels in
Ireland. Those who contributed for this purpose were
called Adventurers. They were to get the Irish confis-
cated lands at the following rates:—In Ulster at 4s.,
in Connaught at 6s., in Munster.at 9s., and in Leinster
at 12s. per acre. Mansions were added free. They had
raised an army of 5,000 foot and 500 horse for the in-
vasion of Munster when the civil war broke out in Eng-
land. Their army was taken to fight against King
Charles I., and never came to Ireland (Cromwellian
Settlement, pp. 72-3-4). Thus the army which was to
subdue the rebels in Ireland was sent to assist the rebels
in England, and those who rebelled against King
Charles I. in England, andcut off his head, got the
lands of Irishmen who had fought for him.

After Cromwell’s war in Ireland many of the dis-
possessed Irish land-holders withdrew to the bogs and
mountains, and were called “Tories.”” They killed
English settlers everywhere they got an opportunity, and
neither gave nor received quarter. They so terrified
the adventurers that they did not come to settle in Ive-
land until 1656-9, when the disbanded soldiers were
settled down on the estates with them to protect them.

1 UR aim is not to drive a flock of human sheep
into an enclosure of views, but to induce
people to grapple intelligently and manfully with the
various questions affecting Irish life, prosperity and
nationality.” :

The extract is taken from the editorial article in the -
first copy of THE LEADER—published well-nigh five years
ago. To my mind, these were noteworthy words in the
Irish political world; and I have always thought that
every man anxious for the truth, no matter to what
sect or party he owed allegiance, should necessarily
subscribe unquestioning assent to the policy which they.
enunciate. In effect they meant that an effort was
about to be made to originate and stimulate thought in
the country. s

The advent of THE LEADER marked a new era
in Irish politics; for not only was it its intention to
encourage people to think for themselves, but, I may
remark parenthetically, it opened a new vista into Irish
life by drawing forth various questions of vital im-
portance to the welfare of the country which the Press
hlthe‘rtq, had, cgnscmusly or unconsciously, allowed to
remain in obscurity. _With regard to self-reliant thought
amongst the people, it is the fact that it had become thi
invariable habit of depending entirely on the Press o

scribe quiescently to the orthodox profession of political
faith according to the party we belonged to, and having
done so we fell into line with our surroundings, and our
power of thinking was allowed to remain in its dormant
_state. This is precisely where we suffered—in lacking
thought. Thus, it was perfectly obvious that the As-
cendancy Party—the Party of great Exclusion—had
become entrenched in a position of unjust predominance
throughout the country, and dared anyone question its
entitlement to that pre-eminence. It was well-known
that thousands of our people were fleeing from Ireland
year after year as from a plague-stricken land. It was
abundantly clear to everyone that our industrial life
was becoming weaker and weaker. Many people
perceived that it appeared- to have become a
second nature to us to receive with unmeasured
applause political speeches leavened by political clap-
trap. These and many similar evils were known to exist,
but such was the system of our training, such the habits
we had grown into, that for years we never drew rein
to apply thought to these questions; we “ buried our
heads in the sand ” and carefully avoided making an
effort “to grapple intelligently and manfully’” with
those questions which concerned so vitally our national
welfare. And it is exactly because the words forming
my text seemed to be the nucleus of a policy that would
correct the evils of our political education by departing
from the system that would ““drive us into an enclosure
of views,’’ and by inducing the people to free individual
thought, that I consider they were epoch-making words
in the world of Irish Politics.

Asuredly he is a benefactor to his country who helps
to bring a greater amount of calm deliberation to bear
on the general conditions of national life and on the
questions that immediately agitate public opinion. The
thoughtful minds in a nation are the brain-force of that
nation; hence the greater the number of thinking

. beings we have the more powerful will be the strength

of the nation’s intellect, and the more rapid the nation’s

- adyancement in prosperity. This is so because, thought

: in the individual, tending towards intellectual
superiority, leads also towards material welfare;
but the laws which govern the individuals

govern likewise the State, and it follows there-
fore that in that state where there is most thought,

-THE LEADER.

875

there is also found most progress. Of the truth of this
contemporary history furnishes ample eyidence.
Germany is the land of thinkers, and Germany is
quickly forging its way to the front in the race for com-
mercial supremacy. In the United States original
thought is assisted and encouraged in every way, and
we know that the United States does not retrogress. On
the other hand, England appears to be on the decline,
and for this the reason is assigned that she is becom-
ing bankrupt in brains, that is bankrupt in thought.
It may be urged that the greater prosperity to be
found in Germany and the United States as compared
with England is attributable to the fact that in those
two countries Universities and Technical Schools are
more numerous and better equipped than we find them
in England. This may be true; but if so, it rather em-
phasises the force of my argument, because if thought
had been as active in England as in the other countries
referred to the English people would surely have dis-
covered and procured the means for the continued
maintenance of their premier position: if Universities
and Technical Schools were the means, those institu-
tions would have been established in England in at
least proportionate numbers to those in rival countries.
Again, so powerful are the forces arrayed against
those striving for the industrial and social advancement
of our own country that in order to achieve any degree-
of success, work is imperatively necessary. Anything,
therefore, that induces us to action possesses in itself
no inconsiderable value. Now nothing more surely
impels us to act than solid conviction; but conviction
is the product of thought only; without thought we
may have mere opinions, but nothing beyond; where,
therefore, as in Ireland, the play of human activities
is essential for the accomplishment of a purpose, it is
perfect clear that the thinking mind is an
asset indispensable to success. The man of
thought in a movement is the architect of that
movement—the constructive force. Infusing en-
thusiasm he controls impetuosity, and is best able to
devise the most effective ways and means for the carry-
ing of a cause to a triumphant issue. If there were no
other consideration we could see from this alone the
salutary influence that must come from the habitual. .
exercise of the nation’s thinking faculties. 3
Ireland has always stood out in history as a land
perpetually torn by internecine strife.  In. the earlier
ages of our country the prevalence of internal wars
need not call forth any special comment, as every coun-
try is in its infancy the victim of such struggles.
In Ireland, furthermore, when there are two peoples
that have never coalesced, and with a well-marked line
of demarcation dividing them, it is only natural to
expect that a disunion more or less troublous would con-
tinue to exist. Still, making proper allowance for such
considerations, I fear it cannot be denied that the occa-
sions on which the unity of the major party, at any
rate, in the country has been destroyed have been
unusually frequent. It seems, further, that we take a
much longer time than other races to restore ourselves
to a condition of amicability, when that has once been
disturbed. When the master genius of Parnell began
to lose its grip on the country, and when men stood up
to throw defiance at him whom they had served so long
and faithfully, we all rushed to take our respective sides
in the struggle that was coming. It was natural, of
-course, that we should divide into opposite camps in
this great crisis. But then, with a fatal facility, we car-
ried on the campaign for a dozen long years. Whatever
effectiveness was possessed by us as a political force we
utterly paralysed. By the continued militant opposi
tion of one party against the other any hope we enter:
tained of seeing previous toil come to some happy frui
tion completely vanished; and it was only after twelv
years of wasted energy that we agreed to let bygon
be bygones, and close up our ranks. Here was disunion
prolonged among us to an altogether unreasonable li; ;
and operating with disastrous consequences to oursely
Now when we come to inquire into the causes that
frequently lead to the destruction of unity and to ﬁg
continuance of the divisions arising thereby, mus? ve
not conclude that much of the. disunion is to be attri-

am

376

buted to a want of thought? Ethnologists may argue
profoundly about “inherent national characteristics”
and the like, but it cannot be denied that every sane
man is capable of exercising his intellect—of bringing
thought to bear on any question—if he so desires. This
is what we have'too often failed to do. Situated as we
are in Ireland, whether we put forth our efforts in the
sphere of moral, social, or industrial reform, the attain-
ment of success demands that we conserve our energy
and concentrate our strength; and knowing full well as
we do that disunion leads immediately to the disintegra-
tion of our forces, do we not act without thought when
we adopt and continue a line of action that results in
nullifying all our endeavours? Most assuredly we do;
and most certainly is it the Tact that if there had always
been more thought amongst us there would have been
less faction. On these considerations, then, it seems a
most palpable truth that that policy is a beneficent, one
which, as defined in the words of my quotation, has for
its object the inducing of the people to utilise their
thinking powers on all those questions which affect our
national life. Indeed, there is not wanting abundant
evidence to show that very considerable benefit has ac-
crued from such a policy after an experience of five
years. In some respects a revolution has been effected,
the national character has been strengthened, and a
healthier condition of public opinion has been brought
into being. Plato would have the legislators of a State
philosophers; Plato was ambitious. If, however, the
people of any country can be got to habituate themselves
t> the practice of calm deliberation in dealing with mat-
ters of national concern, it is pretty certain that quite a
sufficient amount of philosophy will thereby be intro-
duced ifto the administration of the affairs of that
country. CLADDAGH.

LANCING through this Irish Prayer Book recalls
_¥ vividly a time, not so long ago, when the light
of the Revival was but faintly visible on the horizon.
Few noticed it, and fewer still entertained a hope that
the dawn was coming. Father Conway’s ministry was,
at that time, amongst a sturdy Irish-speaking people on
rocky shores of Great Man’s Bay, where, indeed, no
necessity existed for the revival of a language which
old and young spoke in all its vigour and purity. Father
Conway was himself a fluent and effective Irish speaker ;
and to witness on Sundays an exclusively Irish-speaking
congregation in rapt attention listening to his eloquent
discourse would make one oblivious for the time' being
that English influence had any potency over the Irish
mind. The language of Ireland’s saints had also its
rightful place in the prayers offered up before and after
Mass. And at the well-attended evening devotions,
when the feeble taper lights were struggling to outshine
the waning light of day, to see that humble flock and
their good pastor holding commune with  the
Great Unseen in the tones and language of their fore-
fathers in that last unconquered stronghold of the Gael,
awakened an emotion and inspired a hope in the ulti-
mate destiny of a race whose exalted mental traits
neither seven hundred years of devastation nor grinding
penal laws could warp or alter.
I am strongly inclined to consider the little volume
of Irish prayers, which Father Conway now publishes,
